week12

Ben Elliott

2026-04-20

Script Settings and Resources

set.seed(123)            # reproducibility for model training
library(tidyverse)       # data science tools
library(httr)            # data extract from Reddit JSON
library(jsonlite)        # data extract from Reddit JSON
library(tm)              # pre-processing
library(qdap)            # pre-processing # OpenJDK is an open-source solution for these java invoking libraries via University software support
library(textclean)       # pre-processing (emojis)
library(textstem)        # pre-processing
library(RWeka)           # n-gram DTM # OpenJDK is an open-source solution for these java invoking libraries via University software support
library(stm)             # tuning a LDA Model (Topic Extraction)
library(caret)           # machine learning

Data Import and Cleaning

# url <- "https://www.reddit.com/r/IOPsychology/.json?limit=100" # establishes the url, set to limit=100
# after <- NULL
# all_posts <- list()
# 
# for (i in 1:6) {  # full disclosure, I needed an AI solution for this. I struggled trying to use RedditExtractoR because of 429 errors. When I queried AI, I had about three options to try to use in order to get this data; I balanced choices of efficiency, clarity and accuracy. Examining the output from this code, I obtain what I am after regarding a year's worth of posts. I also do not have a Reddit account and therefore wanted a solution that did not require the API calls required with a user/password. 
#   full_url <- paste0(url, if (!is.null(after)) paste0("&after=", after) else "")
#   res <- fromJSON(full_url)
#   posts <- res$data$children$data
#   all_posts[[i]] <- posts
#   after <- res$data$after
#   if (is.null(after)) break
#   Sys.sleep(1)  # important to avoid rate limits
# }
# 
# week12_tbl <- bind_rows(all_posts) %>% # creates week12_tbl
#   select(title, ups, created_utc) %>% # selecting title, upvotes, and date post created
#   mutate(
#     created_utc = as.POSIXct(created_utc, origin = "1970-01-01") # converts to POSIX.ct for date computations
#   ) %>% 
#   filter(created_utc > Sys.Date() - 365) %>% # selects from current to one year back
#   select(-created_utc) %>% # removes date created variable after filtering
#   write_csv("../data/iopsych_scrape_data.csv") # writes to .csv in the data folder

week12_tbl <- read_csv("../data/iopsych_scrape_data.csv") %>%  # reloads, following commenting out
  mutate(doc_id = row_number()) # added a row number to assist with indexing and comparing
## Rows: 419 Columns: 2
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## chr (1): title
## dbl (1): ups
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
doc_ids <- week12_tbl$doc_id # all document ids
# Step 1: Data Wrangling/Munging
io_corpus_original <- VCorpus(VectorSource(week12_tbl$title)) # creates a corups using command from `tm` library

# Step 2: Pre-Processing
io_corpus <- io_corpus_original %>% # preprocessing pipeline
  tm_map(content_transformer(str_to_lower)) %>% #conversion to lower first for consistency
  # tm_map(content_transformer(replace_abbreviation)) %>% # remove abbreviations; commented out because it was overly aggressive
  tm_map(content_transformer(replace_contraction)) %>% # remove contractions
  # tm_map(content_transformer(replace_emoji)) %>% # replace emojis; commented out because it was overly aggressive
  tm_map(removeNumbers) %>% # remove numbers that are not relevant to topics
  tm_map(removePunctuation) %>% # leave out punctuation
  tm_map(content_transformer(function(x) { # this anonymous function removes all the IO Psych-like words using regular expressions and str_replace, identifying the stem and replacing with whitespace (to be removed later)
    x <- str_replace_all(x, "\\bi\\s*o\\b", " ")
    x <- str_replace_all(x, "industrial\\s*organizational", " ")
    x <- str_replace_all(x, "\\bpsych\\w*\\b", " ")
    x <- str_replace_all(x, "riopsychology", " ")
    x
  })) %>% 
tm_map(content_transformer(lemmatize_words)) %>% #from textstem so needs content_transformer() wrapper; #lemmatization of words to conform words
  tm_map(removeWords, c(stopwords("en"))) %>% 
  tm_map(stripWhitespace)   # remove whitespace
  # tm_map(stemDocument, language="english") # remove stems; commented out because it was overly aggressive
# Compare them function created, by setting i as random number within length of corp1 (which in our case is same length as corp2)
compare_them <- function(corp1, corp2) {
  i <- sample(1:length(corp1), 1)
  print(corp1[[i]]$content) # prints content of ith term in corp1
  print(corp2[[i]]$content) # prints content of ith term in corp2
}
compare_them(io_corpus_original, io_corpus) # reran compare_them function several times to evaluate 
## [1] "What are some adjacent job titles I can search for that might not be 100% related to IO but close enough where an I/O MA would still be somewhat relevant"
## [1] " adjacent job titles can search might related close enough ma still somewhat relevant"
# Step 3: Create a Dataset
myTokenizer <- function(x) { NGramTokenizer(x, Weka_control(min=1, max=2)) } # uses tokenization for document term matrix with unigrams and bigrams
io_dtm <- DocumentTermMatrix( # new io_dtm created
  io_corpus, 
  control = list(tokenize = myTokenizer))

# Remove spares terms
io_slim_dtm <- removeSparseTerms(io_dtm, .97) # removes sparsity with reasonable N/k ratio

# Removing empty rows from sparse dtm; aligning with the doc_ids from the original table for further analysis
tokenCounts <- apply(io_slim_dtm, 1, sum)
kept_docs <- tokenCounts > 0
cleaned_dtm <- io_slim_dtm[kept_docs, ]
kept_doc_ids <- doc_ids[kept_docs]

# Ensure text alignment between dtm and original document ids
texts_aligned <- week12_tbl %>%
  filter(doc_id %in% kept_doc_ids) %>%
  arrange(match(doc_id, kept_doc_ids))

## [6,]    1    1    1    1    0    1
## 
## $poscor
##           [,1]      [,2]      [,3]      [,4] [,5]      [,6]
## [1,] 1.0000000 0.3486714 0.8478179 0.5029234    0 0.5523113
## [2,] 0.3486714 1.0000000 0.4526958 0.4827040    0 0.4222457
## [3,] 0.8478179 0.4526958 1.0000000 0.8054622    0 0.8322569
## [4,] 0.5029234 0.4827040 0.8054622 1.0000000    0 0.8567999
## [5,] 0.0000000 0.0000000 0.0000000 0.0000000    1 0.0000000
## [6,] 0.5523113 0.4222457 0.8322569 0.8567999    0 1.0000000
## 
## $cor
##            [,1]       [,2]       [,3]       [,4]       [,5]       [,6]
## [1,]  1.0000000  0.3486714  0.8478179  0.5029234 -0.7363037  0.5523113
## [2,]  0.3486714  1.0000000  0.4526958  0.4827040 -0.6180850  0.4222457
## [3,]  0.8478179  0.4526958  1.0000000  0.8054622 -0.9375478  0.8322569
## [4,]  0.5029234  0.4827040  0.8054622  1.0000000 -0.9329601  0.8567999
## [5,] -0.7363037 -0.6180850 -0.9375478 -0.9329601  1.0000000 -0.8873537
## [6,]  0.5523113  0.4222457  0.8322569  0.8567999 -0.8873537  1.0000000
## 
## attr(,"class")
## [1] "topicCorr"

# Topic Table
topics_tbl <- tibble( 
  doc_id = texts_aligned$doc_id, # doc_ids retained from the original corpus
  original = texts_aligned$title, # original post title
  topic = apply(theta, 1, which.max), # LDA topic number
  topic_label = topic_labels[apply(theta, 1, which.max)], # selects the appropriate topic labels
  probability = apply(theta, 1, max), # the probability that label belongs to the topic
  upvotes = texts_aligned$ups, # the number of upvotes for the document
)

#Q1: Explain in detail the specific reasoning process you used to determine the final number of topics. 
## A1: My specific process is that I ran the model first with 7 topics based on my initial code and the output results of the kresult plot, examining the “elbow” across the four plots. It resulted in many topics that have overlap, both from analyzing the content as well as the empirical correlations. I reduced to 5 topics, but felt as though they became too similar in content overlap. I settled on 6, and while it still feels imperfect, I believe it is suitable given the data pre-processing steps I took (I had done a previous round where I was not as aggressive at removing “psychology” terms and got a large amount of topic flooding from these terms). 
#Q2: Explain in detail the specific reasoning process for the topic labels you selected.
##A2: To label the topics, I first used the plot of the topic models to get a general idea. Then I examined the FREX for unique words in each topic. I began to create labels based on an inductive categorization. It was helpful to look at the original strings to gather nuance. I landed on six labels and then, asked AI to do the same process with my output from line 138 and line 139. I fine tuned my labels slightly. I also feel as though topic 1 & 3 are too closely related, so if I would have had more time I would have examined ways to reduce this or merge the labels (simply going to 5 topics did not help, but only increased the issue).

Visualization

DTM_tbl <- io_dtm %>% as.matrix %>% as_tibble # turns this dataset into a tibble
wordCounts <- colSums(DTM_tbl)
wordNames <- names(DTM_tbl)
wordcloud::wordcloud(wordNames, wordCounts, max.words = 50)
## Warning in wordcloud::wordcloud(wordNames, wordCounts, max.words = 50): reading
## think could not be fit on page. It will not be plotted.
## Warning in wordcloud::wordcloud(wordNames, wordCounts, max.words = 50):
## discussion reading could not be fit on page. It will not be plotted.

## Interpretation: Overall on the original corpus, io_dtm, the word cloud produced demonstrates that this is a forum for networking through seeking and providing advice, help, identifying needs, and talking about general support for the field of I/O Psychology. It is clearly a source for those in applied and academic settings within IO to come to find answers about the questions regarding their school & degree choices, post-graduate early career options, and engage in discussion topics.
